questions.question: What is the ordinary generating function (OGF) of the sequence (1, 1, 1, 1, …)?
questions.explanation: The geometric series 1 + x + x² + x³ + … sums (in the formal-power-series sense) to 1/(1 - x). This is the most-used OGF; many catalog entries derive from it by substitution or differentiation.

questions.question: What does multiplying two OGFs A(x) · B(x) correspond to at the sequence level?
questions.explanation: Power-series multiplication is convolution: to get the coefficient of xⁿ in A·B, sum products aₖ bₙ₋ₖ over all k. This is why OGFs are useful for problems where 'split into two parts' yields a recurrence — the split becomes a product of two series.

questions.question: Fibonacci's recurrence `Fₙ = Fₙ₋₁ + Fₙ₋₂` (F₀=0, F₁=1) yields which OGF?
questions.explanation: Multiply both sides of the recurrence by xⁿ, sum n ≥ 2, recognize Σ Fₙ xⁿ on each side: F(x) - x = x·F(x) + x²·F(x), giving F(x) = x / (1 - x - x²). Partial fractions on this gives Binet's formula Fₙ = (φⁿ - ψⁿ)/√5.

questions.question: The Catalan generating function satisfies which equation?
questions.explanation: From the Catalan recurrence Cₙ₊₁ = Σₖ Cₖ · Cₙ₋ₖ (split at the root / first matching paren), one gets C(x) = 1 + x · C(x)². Solving the quadratic in C(x) gives C(x) = (1 - √(1-4x))/(2x), whose coefficients are Cₙ = (1/(n+1)) C(2n, n).

questions.question: Why is `[xⁿ] (1/(1-x))ᵏ = C(n+k-1, k-1)`?
options: (1/(1-x))ᵏ is the OGF of 'ways to write n as an ordered sum of k non-negative integers' — i.e., stars-and-bars from Lesson 08; the formula C(n+k-1, k-1) is its count.
questions.explanation: Each factor `1/(1-x)` is `1 + x + x² + …` — 'choose xⁱ for some i ≥ 0'. Multiplying k copies gives k independent choices summing to some total n, which is exactly the stars-and-bars count. The OGF view turns combinatorial intuition into algebra and vice versa.

questions.question: What's the OGF of the partition function p(n)?
questions.explanation: Each factor `1/(1 - xᵏ) = 1 + xᵏ + x^(2k) + …` represents 'use the part k any non-negative number of times.' The infinite product captures every possible combination of parts. There's no closed form for p(n), but Hardy-Ramanujan derived asymptotic `p(n) ~ e^(π√(2n/3)) / (4n√3)` from this product.
